title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
objectives: Recognize clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria for DKA
Apply appropriate fluid, insulin, and electrolyte management

vignette.text:
A 24-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her roommate who found her confused and vomiting. She has had 3 days of polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, and abdominal pain. She was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes at age 12 and uses an insulin pump, but admits she ran out of pump supplies 4 days ago and has been 'rationing' insulin. She also had a sore throat and fever for the past week.

Past Medical History: Type 1 DM (12 years, insulin pump, last A1c 8.2%), Hashimoto's thyroiditis

Medications: Insulin lispro via pump (currently not working), Levothyroxine 75mcg daily

Social History: College student, no alcohol or drugs

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Ill-appearing, lethargic, deep rapid breathing, fruity breath odor
physicalExam.HEENT: Dry mucous membranes, pharyngeal erythema with exudates
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, diffusely tender, no peritoneal signs

questions.stem: Type 1 diabetes results from autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing cells.
questions.question: Which cells in the pancreas produce insulin?
questions.hint: These cells are found in clusters called islets.

options.text: Beta cells of the islets of Langerhans
Alpha cells of the islets of Langerhans

questions.explanation: Beta cells produce insulin. In Type 1 DM, autoimmune destruction leads to absolute insulin deficiency. Alpha cells produce glucagon, delta cells produce somatostatin.

sources.text: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 17
StatPearls: Type 1 DM

questions.stem: The liver plays a central role in ketone metabolism.
questions.question: Where in the liver cell does ketogenesis occur?
questions.hint: This organelle is the site of fatty acid oxidation.
questions.explanation: Ketogenesis occurs in liver mitochondria. Fatty acids undergo beta-oxidation to acetyl-CoA, which is converted to ketone bodies when acetyl-CoA exceeds TCA cycle capacity.

sources.text: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 24
StatPearls: Ketogenesis

questions.stem: The patient has Kussmaul respirations.
questions.question: Kussmaul respirations represent compensation by which organ system?

options.text: Respiratory system - hyperventilation to lower pCO2

questions.explanation: Kussmaul respirations are deep, rapid breaths that blow off CO2 to compensate for metabolic acidosis. Expected pCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 = 1.5(8) + 8 = 20 ± 2. Her pCO2 of 18 shows appropriate compensation.

sources.text: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 26
StatPearls: Metabolic Acidosis

questions.stem: Her anion gap is 25 mEq/L.
questions.question: What causes the elevated anion gap in DKA?
questions.explanation: Ketoacids are the unmeasured anions. AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 128 - (95 + 8) = 25. Beta-hydroxybutyrate predominates in DKA (ratio to acetoacetate can be 10:1).

sources.text: StatPearls: Anion Gap
StatPearls: DKA

questions.stem: Her sodium is 128 mEq/L in the setting of hyperglycemia.
questions.question: Calculate the corrected sodium.
questions.hint: Glucose draws water from cells, diluting sodium.

options.text: Add 1.6-2.4 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100; corrected Na ≈ 134-138
Subtract 1.6 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100

questions.explanation: Corrected Na = 128 + 1.6 × (386/100) ≈ 134. The apparent hyponatremia is dilutional. As glucose falls with treatment, measured sodium will rise.

sources.text: StatPearls: DKA
StatPearls: Hyponatremia

questions.stem: Her potassium is 5.8 mEq/L despite total body depletion.
questions.question: Why is serum potassium elevated despite total body depletion?

options.text: Kidneys retain all potassium in DKA
Acidosis and insulin deficiency shift K+ from intracellular to extracellular space

questions.explanation: Acidosis causes H+/K+ exchange across cell membranes. Insulin deficiency prevents K+ uptake into cells. Meanwhile, osmotic diuresis causes massive urinary K+ losses. Total body K+ is depleted despite high serum K+.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient has polyuria despite dehydration.
questions.question: What causes the osmotic diuresis in DKA?
questions.hint: Consider the renal threshold for glucose.

options.text: Glucosuria - glucose exceeds renal reabsorption capacity, pulling water into urine

questions.explanation: When glucose exceeds ~180 mg/dL, it spills into urine. Glucose is osmotically active, obligating water excretion. Average deficit: 6-9L water, 500-700 mEq sodium, 300-500 mEq potassium.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: DKA develops from insulin deficiency plus counter-regulatory hormone excess.
questions.question: What triggers the metabolic cascade leading to ketoacidosis?
questions.hint: Consider what happens when insulin is absent.

options.text: Insulin deficiency → unopposed glucagon → lipolysis → hepatic ketogenesis

questions.explanation: Without insulin, glucagon is unopposed. This activates lipolysis (fat → FFAs) and hepatic ketogenesis (FFAs → ketones). Simultaneously, gluconeogenesis increases, worsening hyperglycemia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: She ran out of insulin supplies and had pharyngitis.
questions.question: Which is the PRIMARY precipitant of her DKA?
questions.hint: Consider the most direct cause of insulin deficiency.

options.text: Insulin omission/pump failure - most common DKA precipitant
Infection is always the primary cause

questions.explanation: Insulin omission accounts for 30-50% of DKA cases. Infection is second most common. She has BOTH - insulin rationing AND strep pharyngitis - a 'double hit.'

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: She has diffuse abdominal pain.
questions.question: What causes abdominal pain in DKA?

options.text: Gastric distension, ileus, and ketone-induced inflammation

questions.explanation: 'DKA belly' - diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and ileus - is common. It typically resolves with DKA treatment. Rule out surgical emergency if peritoneal signs or pain persists.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Her WBC is 16,200.
questions.question: Why is leukocytosis common in DKA without bacterial infection?

options.text: Stress demargination of WBCs and hemoconcentration

questions.explanation: Leukocytosis (10,000-15,000) is common due to stress and hemoconcentration. WBC >25,000 or bandemia suggests true infection. Her positive strep test confirms actual infection.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Initial treatment includes fluid resuscitation.
questions.question: What is the recommended initial IV fluid for DKA?

options.text: 0.9% Normal Saline - 1-1.5 L in first hour

questions.explanation: Start NS 1-1.5 L in first hour. Then adjust: if corrected Na high/normal → 0.45% NS; if low → continue NS. Add dextrose when glucose reaches 200-250 mg/dL to allow continued insulin infusion.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Insulin is the cornerstone of DKA treatment.
questions.question: What is the recommended insulin regimen?
questions.explanation: Regular insulin 0.1 u/kg/hr continuous IV. Can give 0.1 u/kg bolus initially. Reduce to 0.02-0.05 u/kg/hr when glucose reaches 200. Hold insulin only if K+ <3.3 mEq/L.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Her potassium is 5.8 mEq/L.
questions.question: When should potassium replacement begin?
questions.hint: Potassium will fall rapidly with treatment.

options.text: Start KCl when K+ falls below 5.3 mEq/L with adequate urine output

questions.explanation: Add KCl 20-40 mEq/L when K+ <5.3 mEq/L. Goal: maintain K+ 4-5 mEq/L. Despite initial hyperkalemia, total body K+ is depleted. Average deficit: 3-5 mEq/kg.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Her pH is 7.12.
questions.question: Should sodium bicarbonate be given?

options.text: Generally NO; consider only if pH <6.9 or severe hyperkalemia
Yes, give to all patients with pH <7.3
Aggressive bicarbonate is mainstay of treatment

questions.explanation: Routine bicarbonate is NOT recommended. It may worsen intracellular acidosis. Consider only if pH <6.9, severe hyperkalemia, or refractory shock. Acidosis self-corrects with DKA treatment.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: She has strep pharyngitis.
questions.question: What antibiotic is first-line for Group A strep pharyngitis?
questions.hint: GAS remains universally susceptible to this antibiotic.
questions.explanation: Penicillin or amoxicillin is first-line. GAS has never developed penicillin resistance. Treatment addresses both infection and a DKA precipitant.

sources.text: IDSA Pharyngitis Guidelines
StatPearls: GAS Pharyngitis

questions.stem: Confirming DKA requires specific criteria.
questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for DKA?

options.text: Glucose >250, pH <7.3 or HCO3 <18, positive ketones, elevated AG

questions.explanation: DKA: Hyperglycemia (>250), Acidosis (pH <7.3 or HCO3 <18), Ketosis (positive ketones or BHB >3). Severity by pH: mild >7.25, moderate 7.0-7.24, severe <7.0. She has moderate DKA (pH 7.12).

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: DKA and HHS have different presentations.
questions.question: How does HHS differ from DKA?
questions.hint: Consider the degree of hyperglycemia and ketosis.

options.text: HHS always occurs in Type 1 diabetes
HHS and DKA are identical conditions
HHS has more ketosis than DKA

questions.explanation: HHS: glucose usually >600, pH >7.3 (minimal ketosis), osmolality >320, more profound dehydration. Typically Type 2 DM. Can have overlap. Treatment similar but HHS needs more fluids.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Resolution criteria guide when to transition to subcutaneous insulin.
questions.question: What defines DKA resolution?

options.text: Glucose <200, pH >7.3, HCO3 ≥15, anion gap <12, patient eating

questions.explanation: Resolution requires: glucose <200 AND two of: pH >7.3, HCO3 ≥15, AG <12. Also patient should be eating. Overlap subcutaneous insulin 1-2 hours before stopping IV insulin to prevent rebound hyperglycemia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Monitoring frequency is critical in DKA management.
questions.question: How often should labs be monitored during DKA treatment?

options.text: Basic metabolic panel every 2-4 hours initially

questions.explanation: Check BMP every 2-4 hours initially (including glucose, K+, HCO3, AG). Glucose can be checked hourly via glucometer. Adjust fluids, insulin, and K+ based on results. Frequency can decrease as DKA resolves.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The anion gap helps track DKA resolution.
questions.question: Why is following the anion gap important?

options.text: AG closure indicates ketoacid metabolism and true DKA resolution
AG is not useful in monitoring
AG changes don't correlate with clinical status

questions.explanation: AG closure (return to <12) indicates ketoacids have been metabolized. Glucose may normalize before AG closes. Continue insulin until AG closes to fully clear ketosis, using dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: During treatment, her potassium drops to 2.8 mEq/L.
questions.question: What is the most dangerous complication of rapidly falling potassium?
questions.explanation: Severe hypokalemia causes life-threatening arrhythmias (VT, VF). ECG shows U waves, flattened T waves, prolonged QT. This is why K+ monitoring and replacement are critical. Hold insulin if K+ <3.3.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A young patient with DKA develops headache, confusion, and vomiting during treatment.
questions.question: What life-threatening complication should you suspect?
questions.hint: This primarily affects children and young adults.
questions.explanation: Cerebral edema is rare but devastating (mortality 20-25%), primarily in children/adolescents. Risk factors: rapid fluid administration, rapid glucose correction, bicarbonate use, severe acidosis. Treat with mannitol or hypertonic saline.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: After DKA resolves, you're transitioning to subcutaneous insulin.
questions.question: What is the most common error when transitioning from IV to subcutaneous insulin?

options.text: Using the wrong type of insulin
Stopping IV insulin before subcutaneous insulin takes effect

questions.explanation: IV insulin has a 5-minute half-life. Subcutaneous long-acting insulin takes 2-4 hours for effect. Overlap is essential: give subcutaneous basal insulin 1-2 hours BEFORE stopping IV insulin to prevent rebound hyperglycemia/ketosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient develops a non-anion gap acidosis during treatment.
questions.question: What causes non-anion gap metabolic acidosis during DKA treatment?
questions.hint: Consider what happens to chloride with NS infusion.

options.text: Hyperchloremic acidosis from large volume NS infusion

questions.explanation: Large NS volumes cause hyperchloremic (non-AG) metabolic acidosis. The AG closes as ketoacids clear, but pH may remain low due to chloride excess. This is usually self-limited and doesn't require specific treatment.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Integrating all clinical information.
questions.question: Which statement BEST synthesizes this patient's presentation?
questions.hint: Connect precipitants, presentation, and management priorities.

options.text: Type 1 diabetic with moderate DKA precipitated by insulin omission + strep pharyngitis, requiring fluids, insulin, K+ monitoring, and antibiotics
Type 2 diabetic with HHS requiring only fluids

questions.explanation: This synthesis captures: Type 1 DM substrate, dual precipitants (pump failure + infection), severity (moderate DKA by pH 7.12), and management priorities (IV fluids, insulin infusion, K+ replacement, strep treatment).

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Planning discharge and prevention.
questions.question: What is the most important intervention to prevent recurrent DKA?

options.text: Education on sick-day management and ensuring insulin/supply access

questions.explanation: Recurrent DKA is often preventable. Key interventions: (1) Sick-day rules (never stop insulin, increase monitoring); (2) Address insulin access/affordability; (3) Backup supplies for pump failure; (4) Close follow-up with diabetes educator.

sources.text: StatPearls: Diabetic Ketoacidosis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Kitabchi AE, et al. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adult Patients With Diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2009;32(7):1335-1343. Accessed January 14, 2026.
